item.title: Day 10 — The War Spreads: Iraq, Oil, and Trump's Impossible Choice
item.description:
What happened today

Day 10 brought the war off the naval battlefield and into the oil fields of Iraq. Iranian-backed proxies launched coordinated Shahed drone strikes against critical energy infrastructure in Basra province — and for the first time, the conflict directly threatened Western corporate assets and global oil supply in a way no insurance actuary had modeled.

The Basra Strikes

  • Iranian proxy militias in Iraq attacked Halliburton facilities in Basra with Shahed drones, triggering fires at the oil services compound. (Reuters)
  • The Rumaila oil field — one of Iraq’s largest, operated by British Petroleum — was struck by a drone attack. Rumaila produces roughly 1.4 million barrels per day; any disruption cascades directly into global markets. (Long War Journal)
  • Baghdad International Airport and Victoria Base were attacked again, the fourth consecutive day of strikes on coalition logistics infrastructure. (Field Reports)

The Naval & Electronic War

item.title: Day 9 — The Chokepoint: Iran's Selective Naval Blockade and the Battle for the Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
What happened today

The war entered a new phase on Day 9 — not in the skies over Isfahan or Natanz, but on the water. The Strait of Hormuz, the 33-kilometer-wide chokepoint through which 21% of the world’s traded oil passes (IEA), became the day’s defining battlefield.

The Hormuz Gambit

  • Iran’s IRGC announced that the Strait of Hormuz has not been formally closed — but vessels with links to the United States or Israel will not be permitted to transit. (IRNA)
  • This is a legal and strategic novelty: a selective, discriminatory blockade based on flag and ownership rather than a total closure. Maritime lawyers worldwide are calling it unprecedented under UNCLOS.
  • Iran’s IRGC spokesman dared President Trump directly: “Send your Navy to escort the tankers. We are waiting.”

The Naval Exchange

item.title: Day 8 — The Fog of War: Information Warfare and Competing Narratives
item.description:
What happened today

  • Iran’s state broadcaster IRIB aired footage of undamaged centrifuge halls at Natanz — images analysts say are at least six months old. (Reuters)
  • The IDF Spokesperson Unit released a 90-second cinematic video titled “Operation Iron Verdict”, with orchestral soundtrack and Hebrew captions, streamed directly to 4.2 million YouTube subscribers. (IDF Spokesperson Unit)
  • Pentagon Press Secretary read a 340-word statement at today’s briefing, took eleven questions, and confirmed zero operational details. Three journalists walked out. (Pentagon Press Briefing)
  • On Telegram, pro-Iranian channels gained 800,000 new subscribers in 24 hours, primarily from Arabic-speaking users in Iraq and Lebanon.
  • X/Twitter suspended 47 accounts flagged as Iranian state-linked influence networks. (Reuters) Iranian state media called the suspensions “digital aggression.”

The Three Narratives

Every belligerent in this conflict has a story to tell, and each story serves a domestic audience first.

item.title: Prophetic Analysis — The War in Light of Ancient Texts
item.description:
Introduction

This page is not a theological argument. It is a framework for pattern recognition.

Ancient civilizations — across cultures, continents, and centuries — developed prophetic traditions that described the end of one age and the birth of another. Those traditions use different languages, different cosmologies, different figures. But they converge on a structure: mounting chaos, a great war, moral collapse, and ultimately transformation.

The war that began on February 28, 2026 has activated more prophetic tripwires in nine days than most historians would expect in a decade. We document them here — not to claim that the end is near, but because the patterns are real and the connections deserve honest examination.

item.title: Day 7 — Iran Rejects All Talks; Trump Eyes Iran's Next Leader
item.description:
What happened today

  • Iran’s Foreign Minister Araghchi stated publicly: “No ceasefire, no negotiations.” Iran will not come to the table.
  • US and Israeli strikes hit military academies and infrastructure targets inside Iran.
  • Trump reiterated his intention to personally have a say in who becomes Iran’s next Supreme Leader — a direct statement of regime change as a war goal.
  • Russia continues to observe without acting. The Suwalki Gap (Poland–Lithuania corridor, Russia’s potential entry point into Europe) remains quiet.
  • Markets reflect a war with no end in sight: Bitcoin ~$71,200 / Brent ~$125 / Gold ~$3,200. (Bloomberg)

Intelligence & Field Reports

  • Iranian FM Araghchi confirmed publicly: “We haven’t asked for a ceasefire and we reject any negotiations with America.” — A direct statement eliminating any diplomatic off-ramp. (AFP)
  • Russian President Putin held a call with Iranian President Pezeshkian, calling for an immediate ceasefire in the Middle East — while simultaneously, US officials report Russia is providing Iran with intelligence on American warship and aircraft positions. (TASS)
  • Israel’s Channel 11 reported that Azerbaijan may soon enter the war on Israel’s side, with Israeli media fabricating a formal pretext for an Azerbaijani attack on Iran. Iran was specifically warned to prepare for this scenario. (Times of Israel)
  • Trump demanded the US military-industrial complex quadruple weapons and ammunition production — a striking reversal after claiming just one day earlier that US reserves were “practically endless.” (Reuters)
  • Iran destroyed a key $300 million US radar system used to direct missile defense operations in the Gulf — a significant degradation of America’s defensive architecture in the theater. (Bloomberg)

Key numbers

Metric Value
Bitcoin ~$71,200
Brent crude ~$125/barrel
Gold ~$3,200/oz
Hormuz Strait Critical
Russia (Suwalki) Quiet — watching
Strategic escalation 9 / 10

Where things stand

One week in, neither side is backing down. Iran has refused negotiations. The US has made regime change an explicit goal. Strikes continue on both sides. Russia is biding its time. The global economy is absorbing $125 oil — for now. The next big threshold is Damascus: if Israel hits Syria, Russia’s calculus may change.

item.title: Day 6 — US Sinks Iranian Warship in the Persian Gulf
item.description:
What happened today

  • The US Navy sank an Iranian warship in the Persian Gulf. This is the first major naval engagement of the war and a direct escalation on the sea. (Pentagon)
  • Iran’s government immediately announced retaliation would follow.
  • Trump publicly rejected Khamenei’s son as a potential successor to Iran’s Supreme Leader — and said the US intends to have a say in who leads Iran next.
  • Israel struck Hezbollah targets in Beirut’s southern suburbs again.
  • Damascus remains intact. Syria has not been drawn in yet.

Intelligence & Field Reports

  • The US military reported a cumulative total of 43 Iranian naval vessels sunk since the start of the conflict — a figure encompassing both warships and smaller fast-attack craft. (Pentagon)
  • Satellite imagery showed severe destruction at US Camp Arifjan military base in Kuwait following Iranian retaliatory missile strikes — including confirmed damage to hangars and logistics infrastructure.
  • The US State Department suspended operations at the American Embassy in Kuwait City, citing ongoing security concerns — one of the first formal diplomatic casualties of the conflict. (State Department)
  • Wealthy investors are quietly withdrawing capital from Dubai: multiple family offices with average holdings of $50M+ each inquired about relocating to Singapore or Hong Kong within a single week, per Reuters-cited wealth advisors. The Gulf’s “safe haven” status is evaporating. (Reuters)
  • Kuwait began cutting oil production after running out of storage capacity for its 2.6 million barrels/day output — a direct consequence of the Hormuz blockade disrupting Gulf export routes. (Bloomberg)

Key numbers

Metric Value
Iranian warships sunk 1
Hormuz Strait Critical
Strategic escalation 9 / 10
Damascus Still intact

Where things stand

The war just went naval. Sinking a warship is a qualitative leap — it signals the US is willing to destroy Iran’s military hardware, not just its land infrastructure. Trump’s comments about Iran’s leadership succession are extraordinary: the US is openly talking about regime change. The Strait of Hormuz is now at critical status.

item.title: Day 5 — 500 Missiles, 2,000 Drones: Trump Vows 24/7 Strikes
item.description:
What happened today

  • Five days in, the running total is staggering: Iran has fired 500+ ballistic missiles and launched roughly 2,000 drones. About 40% targeted Israel; 60% targeted US positions. (Pentagon)
  • The IRGC officially confirmed it attacked Nakhchivan (Azerbaijan) — the first time Iran acknowledged cross-border strikes on an ex-Soviet state. (IRNA)
  • Trump posted: “24/7 strikes — seabed to space and cyberspace.” No domain is off limits. (Truth Social)
  • Bitcoin has now fallen 15% since the war started; gold is up 12%. (Bloomberg)
  • The Strait of Hormuz remains under partial blockade. Energy markets are in sustained stress.

Intelligence & Field Reports

  • US officials confirmed to the Washington Post that Russia is providing Iran with real-time intelligence on the positions of American warships, aircraft, and radar systems in the Middle East — making Russia an indirect participant in the conflict. China was not reported to be providing similar assistance. (Washington Post)
  • The USS Abraham Lincoln aircraft carrier reportedly withdrew from the active theater under pressure from Iranian missiles and drones. Iran’s parliament speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf mocked the retreat: “The famous bride left the battlefield at the first encounter with Iranian missiles and drones.” (IRNA)
  • The IRGC spokesman publicly dared the US to send its Navy to escort oil tankers through the Strait of Hormuz — a direct challenge signaling Iran’s confidence in its ability to threaten naval traffic. (IRNA)
  • Iran’s Ministry of Defense vowed to deploy “more advanced weapons” in coming days, promising “good news” for the Iranian nation. (IRNA)

Key numbers

Metric Value
Ballistic missiles fired (total) 500+
Drones launched (total) ~2,000
Bitcoin change (war total) -15%
Gold change (war total) +12%
Hormuz Strait Partial blockade
Strategic escalation 8 / 10

Where things stand

Iran is burning through its missile inventory at a high rate, but it has not stopped. US and Israeli missile defenses are absorbing the strikes, but it takes resources and time. Trump’s “24/7” declaration signals the US will not pause for negotiations. Financial markets are pricing in a long war.

item.title: Day 4 — Beirut Bombed, 1,230+ Dead in Iran
item.description:
What happened today

  • Israel launched airstrikes on Hezbollah infrastructure in the southern suburbs of Beirut (the Dahieh district).
  • The cumulative death toll inside Iran reached at least 1,230 (Reuters); Israeli losses stand at 11 (IDF Spokesperson Unit); Gulf states have reported 9+ dead.
  • The US Senate voted on a War Powers Resolution to curtail the President’s authority — it was rejected. The war continues with full legal backing. (Reuters)
  • Oil hit Brent $120/barrel for the first time since the 2022 energy crisis. (Bloomberg)
  • The Strait of Hormuz remains under partial blockade.

Intelligence & Field Reports

  • Satellite imagery confirmed a large fire at the Fujairah Oil Industry Zone in the UAE, following an Iranian strike the previous day — the first confirmed Iranian strike on UAE territory, adding a new state to the conflict’s geography. (Reuters)
  • Iran launched a fresh wave of ballistic missiles at Israel on Day 4, maintaining offensive tempo despite mounting losses inside Iran. (IDF Spokesperson Unit)
  • European nations are moving from passive to active support of the US-Israeli campaign: economic, diplomatic, and limited military assistance is being provided despite official EU calls for restraint — contradicting the bloc’s public neutrality.
  • A US stealth pilot departing from Israel’s Ovda Air Base forgot to disable his transponder and was tracked over Saudi Arabia via Flightradar24 — revealing the route of US strikes and potentially compromising operational security.

Key numbers

Metric Value
Iranian deaths (cumulative) 1,230+
Israeli deaths 11
Gulf state deaths 9+
Brent crude >$120/barrel
Hormuz Strait Partial blockade
Strategic escalation 8 / 10

Where things stand

The war has crossed into Lebanon. Israel is dealing with Iran and Hezbollah simultaneously. The death toll inside Iran is rising rapidly, but the regime shows no sign of backing down. Oil at $120 is starting to hit global supply chains. The US Senate’s rejection of the War Powers Resolution gives the White House a free hand.

item.title: Day 3 — Iran Hits US Bases in Azerbaijan and Qatar
item.description:
What happened today

  • Iran attacked US military bases in Azerbaijan and Qatar — expanding the war beyond Israel’s borders.
  • Six US soldiers were killed in these strikes — the first confirmed American combat deaths of the conflict. (Pentagon)
  • Airspace over the entire Gulf region was disrupted; commercial flights rerouted or cancelled.
  • Protests broke out in Tehran — ordinary Iranians taking to the streets against the regime even as the war intensifies.
  • Iran’s partial blockade of the Strait of Hormuz has begun.

Intelligence & Field Reports

  • A senior IRGC General warned: *“Within a few days, US bases in the Middle East will have no way to defend against incoming Iranian missiles and drones.” (IRNA)
  • The Rotana Hotel in Erbil, Iraqi Kurdistan, was struck by an Iranian drone — confirming Iran’s willingness to target civilian-adjacent infrastructure across multiple countries beyond the direct combat zone. (Reuters)
  • Qatar announced partial resumption of air traffic, following the Gulf-wide airspace disruption triggered by Day 3 Iranian strikes — signaling the conflict’s direct impact on regional commercial aviation. (Reuters)
  • Iran struck additional targets in Iraqi Kurdistan and the Persian Gulf on the same day, broadening its operational theater beyond Azerbaijan and Qatar. (IRNA)

Key numbers

Metric Value
US soldiers killed 6
Hormuz Strait Partial blockade
Airspace Disrupted (Gulf-wide)
Strategic escalation 7 / 10

Where things stand

Iran has chosen to go wide rather than deep — striking US infrastructure across two countries to stretch American response capacity. The first American deaths will increase domestic pressure on the White House to hit harder. Meanwhile, the regime faces its own population on the streets of Tehran.

item.title: Day 2 — Iran Fires First Ballistic Missiles
item.description:
What happened today

  • Iran fired its first ballistic missiles at US military bases in the region. Israel’s Iron Dome and Arrow missile defense systems intercepted most of them.
  • CENTCOM confirmed its force posture: 50,000 troops, 200 aircraft, and two aircraft carriers — the Gerald R. Ford and Eisenhower — in the theater. (CENTCOM)
  • Hezbollah began firing rockets from Lebanon into Northern Israel, opening a second front.
  • Iran’s cyber units launched attacks against Israeli and US energy and communications infrastructure.
  • The Strait of Hormuz remains open but is now under tension.

Intelligence & Field Reports

  • Iran’s Ministry of Defense stated in the evening that US and Israeli military casualties are significantly higher than publicly acknowledged; ambulances were continuously delivering bodies and wounded from areas hit by Iranian missiles — particularly US facilities in Bahrain. Iran claimed its own military losses are “significantly lower.” (IRNA)
  • Satellite imagery confirmed at least 20 direct hits on the US Ali Al Salem Air Base in Kuwait, with a Patriot air defense system, hangars, radars, and other military structures reportedly struck.
  • Iran officially stated: “The Strait of Hormuz is not closed — but ships linked to the US or Israel will not be allowed to pass.” A selective blockade, not a total closure — a precise legal and military maneuver. (IRNA)
  • Saudi Arabia intensified backchannel diplomatic talks with Iran in an effort to defuse the conflict, according to Bloomberg — despite Riyadh’s official public silence. (Bloomberg)

Key numbers

Metric Value
US troops in region 50,000
US aircraft 200
US carriers 2 (Ford + Eisenhower)
Hormuz Strait Tense
Strategic escalation 7 / 10

Where things stand

Iran has moved from drones to ballistic missiles — a significant escalation. US missile defenses are holding, but the volume of incoming fire is rising. Hezbollah’s activation means Israel is now fighting on two fronts simultaneously. Cyber attacks are a third dimension.

item.title: Day 1 — USA & Israel Launch Airstrikes on Iran
item.description:
What happened today

  • The United States and Israel launched coordinated airstrikes targeting Iran’s IRGC (Revolutionary Guards), missile storage sites, and nuclear infrastructure.
  • Iran began mobilizing forces and fired the first drones in response — most were shot down.
  • The UN Security Council held an emergency session but reached no agreement; Russia and China vetoed any resolution. (Reuters)
  • Oil prices jumped 8% within hours of the strikes being confirmed. (Reuters)
  • Bitcoin dropped 5% as risk-off sentiment hit markets. (Bloomberg)

Intelligence & Field Reports

  • Trump announced “Operation Epic Fury” via a video posted to Truth Social at 2:30 AM Washington time on February 28, 2026 — the first public declaration of the strikes before mainstream media confirmed. (Truth Social)
  • Independent analysts note that the Iran campaign follows a recognizable pattern: Iraq (2003), Syria (2011), Libya (2011), Somalia, Sudan — Iran is described as the final entry in a decade-long series of US military campaigns targeting nations on a pre-existing list.
  • Russia and China vetoed the UN Security Council resolution, publicly confirming their bloc against Western military action in the region — a geopolitical alignment with direct implications for the next phase. (Reuters)

Key numbers

Metric Value
Oil price change +8%
Bitcoin -5%
Hormuz Strait Open
Strategic escalation 6 / 10

Where things stand

The war has started. US and Israeli aircraft struck Iran’s most sensitive military sites simultaneously. Iran’s air defenses responded but were largely overwhelmed. The Strait of Hormuz remains open — for now. The UN is paralyzed by vetoes.

Iran/USA/Israel War 2026 — Daily Chronicle

Last updated: 07.03.2026 | Sources: Reuters, AP, BBC, Al Jazeera, NBC, Wikipedia QA-Review: Opus (07.03.2026)

Daily Overview

Day Date Main Event Hormuz Status Strategic Index
1 28.02.2026 USA/Israel begin airstrikes on Iran Open 6/10
2 01.03.2026 Iran fires first waves of missiles/drones Tense 7/10
3 02.03.2026 Attacks on US bases in Azerbaijan/Qatar Partial Blockade 7/10
4 03.03.2026 Beirut bombarded, Hezbollah active Partial Blockade 8/10
5 04.03.2026 500+ missiles, 2,000+ drones cumulative Partial Blockade 8/10
6 05.03.2026 USA sinks Iranian warship Critical 9/10
7 06.03.2026 Iran rejects negotiations; Trump wants to determine Iran’s leader Critical 9/10
8 07.03.2026 Operation Epic Fury (Day 5); Tehran warns Europe; $151.8M ammunition for Israel Critical 9/10

Detailed Chronicle

Day 1 — February 28, 2026

Category Details
Military USA + Israel launch coordinated airstrikes on IRGC, missile depots, nuclear infrastructure
Iran Mobilization; first drone intercepts
Diplomacy UN Security Council meets — no agreement (Russia/China veto)
Economy Oil price +8%, Bitcoin -5%

| Russia | Observing, no movements |
